channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Trump, Defiant After Bad Week, Pushes Ahead on Politically Unpopular Ideas
item.description: President Trump continues to act like he’s politically all-powerful, even in the face of indications that he is not.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater
item.media:description: President Trump at the White House on Friday.

item.title: Green Card Seekers Must Leave U.S. to Apply, Trump Administration Says
item.description: The change is likely to affect hundreds of thousands of people. It could also lead to more family separations as spouses or relatives wait for application decisions, immigration lawyers said.
item.dc:creator: Madeleine Ngo and Albert Sun
item.media:credit: Libby March for The New York Times
item.media:description: There are various pathways for foreigners to obtain green cards, which grant them the ability to live and work in the United States as permanent residents.

item.title: Tulsi Gabbard Resigns as Director of National Intelligence. Trump Names Aaron Lukas Acting Chief.
item.description: Ms. Gabbard had a difficult tenure in the Trump administration and was seldom seen in the room when the president made important national-security moves.
item.dc:creator: Dustin Volz and Julian E. Barnes
item.media:description: Tulsi Gabbard at a House hearing in March. She said on Friday that she would remain in her post as the nation’s intelligence chief until June 30.

item.title: Once Trump’s Co-Pilot Against Iran, Netanyahu Is Now a Mere Passenger
item.description: A partner in the war, Israel has been largely left out of the peace talks, a humbling setback for its prime minister with significant risks for the country.
item.dc:creator: David M. Halbfinger and Ronen Bergman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu of Israel with President Trump at Mar-a-Lago in December.

item.title: Israeli Strikes Pummel Lebanon, Killing Medics Amid Fragile Truce
item.description: Saturday’s strikes damaged a main hospital in the Lebanese city of Tyre, as funerals for paramedics killed a day earlier were held.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Chaayto and Abdi Latif Dahir
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Mourners at the funeral of a paramedic and two civil defense workers, in Tyre, Lebanon, on Saturday. They were killed in an Israeli airstrike the day before, according to Lebanon’s health ministry.

item.title: Iran and U.S. Officials Signal Progress as Cease-Fire Hangs in Balance
item.description: As people across the Middle East braced for the possibility of renewed fighting, officials from both sides said there were signs that they were moving closer to an agreement.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman and Leily Nikounazar
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A handout picture from the Islamic Consultative Assembly News Agency, showing Syed Asim Munir, Pakistan’s army chief of staff, left, meeting with Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, Iran’s chief negotiator and parliamentary speaker, on Saturday in Tehran.

item.title: Short Naps, Long Hours: How Autism Clinics Squeeze Medicaid Dollars Out of Preschoolers
item.description: The industry has grown rapidly, straining state budgets. A focus on finances has led to overbilling, fraud and even harm.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Kliff, Margot Sanger-Katz, Erin Schaff and Asmaa Elkeurti

item.title: 5 Takeaways From a Times Investigation on Autism Therapy Clinics
item.description: A rapidly growing industry often overprescribes treatment to young children with autism, who spend as many as 40 hours a week at the facilities.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Kliff, Margot Sanger-Katz and Asmaa Elkeurti
item.media:description: A clinic for autistic children in Concord, N.C., that uses applied behavioral analysis, one of the largest and fastest-growing kinds of medical care for state Medicaid programs.

item.title: What to Know About the Garden Grove Chemical Plant Crisis
item.description: Firefighters continue to cool a chemical storage tank with water to prevent it from breaching or exploding. Some 40,000 residents near the Orange County plant have been evacuated to shelters.
item.dc:creator: Soumya Karlamangla and Pooja Salhotra
item.media:credit: Ethan Swope/Associated Press, via Ap Photo/Ethan Swope
item.media:description: The GKN Aerospace site where a chemical storage tank is at risk of exploding.

item.title: Xi Calls for All-Out Rescue After Coal Mine Explosion Kills at Least 90 in China
item.description: The death count rose drastically on Saturday as the Chinese leader, Xi Jinping, called for an investigation and emphasized the need to “hold those responsible to account.”
item.dc:creator: Catie Edmondson
item.media:description: Rescuers at the site of a deadly explosion at the Liushenyu coal mine in China’s Shanxi Province on Saturday.

item.title: U.S. Ebola Travel Ban Faces Criticism From Congo Health Officials
item.description: Kinshasa residents continue to pack markets, bars and public transportation, despite growing international concern about the spread of the virus.
item.dc:creator: Justin Makangara and John Eligon
item.media:credit: Justin Makangara for The New York Times
item.media:description: A crowded market on Saturday in Kinshasa, the Congolese capital.

item.title: Mob Burns Congo Ebola Center Amid Rare Strain Outbreak
item.description: Several hundred people massed at the gates of a hospital to demand the body of a suspected Ebola victim. Violence broke out when staff refused the request.
item.dc:creator: Declan Walsh and Arlette Bashizi
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times

item.title: White House Tells Congo’s Soccer Team to Isolate, Citing Ebola Outbreak
item.description: The team, now in Belgium, must isolate there for 21 days or risk being denied permission to come to Houston for the World Cup, an official said.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses
item.media:description: The Democratic Republic of Congo national soccer team before a World Cup qualifying match against Cameroon in November, in Rabat, Morocco.

item.title: Nicolas Cage Made Himself a Legend. Then He Had to Live With It.
item.description: The iconic actor on becoming a meme, a myth and larger than life.
item.dc:creator: David Marchese
item.media:credit: Devin Oktar Yalkin for The New York Times

item.title: Best Memorial Day Recipes
item.description: Strawberry shortcake, pasta salad, coleslaw and more recipes to kick off summer. Here’s what to make for picnics, barbecues and gatherings this long weekend.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:credit: Rachel Vanni for The New York Times. Food Stylist: Spencer Richards.

item.title: Memorial Day Travel Forecast: Gas Prices, Traffic and Flights
item.description: Most U.S. travelers will be driving to their destinations for the long weekend, kicking off what is expected to be a busy summer travel season.
item.dc:creator: Christine Chung
item.media:description: A record 45 million Americans are expected to hit the highways over the long weekend, according to AAA.

item.title: Now Is Not the Time to Play Political Make-Believe
item.description: Voters want to dictate which offices their preferred politicians hold, what positions they take, and even where and how they live. It is fantasy football: political edition.
item.dc:creator: Roxane Gay

item.title: Has Trump Gone Full ‘Mob Boss’?
item.description: The president’s $1.8 billion slush fund is causing further cracks in the Republican Party.
item.dc:creator: Jamelle Bouie, Michelle Cottle, David French and Derek Arthur
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
House Select Committee to Investigate the January 6th Attack
House Select Subcommittee on the Weaponization of the Federal Government
item.media:credit: Illustration by The New York Times; photo by Brendan Smialowski/Getty

item.title: Track Changes
item.description: It’s a holiday weekend, three days to ease from one seasonal mind-set into the next, even if it feels too soon.
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch

item.title: ‘A Total Nightmare’: Voices From a Moscow Hit by Ukrainian Drones
item.description: Residents who experienced attacks in recent days were shaken deeply by the conflict coming home to the capital region, the seat of the Kremlin’s power.
item.dc:creator: Valerie Hopkins and Nanna Heitmann
item.media:description: A damaged residential building in Zelenograd, Russia, after a drone strike on Sunday.

item.title: Their Phones Were Stolen in London. Then the Threats Started.
item.description: Tens of thousands of smartphones were reported stolen in the British capital in recent years. For some victims, losing their phone was only the beginning.
item.dc:creator: Lizzie Dearden and Amelia Nierenberg
item.media:credit: Joshua Lott for The New York Times
item.media:description: Alex Pikula at home in Chicago. He had his phone stolen while on a trip to London in May 2024, after which his mother started receiving strange and increasingly threatening texts.

item.title: How ‘Sentinel Gardens’ Help Spot Dangerous Bugs Abroad
item.description: Scientists have planted American trees in China, Korea and elsewhere to attract hungry insects. Their hope is to identify the most damaging bugs before they cross the ocean.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey
item.media:description: A citrus long-horned beetle on a sugar maple in a “sentinel garden” in China.
